docs.title: 🐝 CodeHive Knowledge Base
docs.text: Welcome to the official project documentation for CodeHive. This site is a centralized repository for our technical designs, server configurations, and development logs.

docs.title: 📁 Active Projects
docs.text:
We organize our work by individual project modules. Each project contains its own set of technical specs, deployment guides, and contributor logs.

  • Minecraft CPU
    • Status: Active Development
    • Goal: Build a functional 8-bit computer within a virtual minecraft environment using redstone engineering.
    • Learning Objective : Computer Infrastructure & Engineering

docs.title: 💬 Communication & Community
docs.text: We use Discord as our primary hub for real-time collaboration, debugging sessions, and general announcements. * Invite Link: https://discord.gg/Z8b9nCgNGZ

docs.title: 🛠️ Global Tools & Infrastructure
docs.text:
We maintain a standard set of tools across all projects to ensure efficiency: * Environment Management: We use uv for Python dependency management and virtual environments. * Virtualization: Docker is our primary deployment method for self-hosted services. * Documentation: This site is built with MkDocs Material and automatically deployed via GitHub Actions.

Note to Members: Before starting a new project or contributing, please read the Onboarding Guide.

docs.title: 📥 Onboarding & Contribution
docs.text: Welcome to CodeHive. To maintain a high standard of technical documentation, we use a tiered contribution system based on your role and experience level.

docs.title: 🛠️ Environment Setup
docs.text:
All members contributing to the documentation must use uv for fast, reliable Python dependency management.

  1. Install uv: Follow the official installation guide.
  2. Clone the Repo: bash git clone [https://github.com/codehive/project-docs.git](https://github.com/codehive/project-docs.git)
  3. Sync Environment: Navigate to the project folder and run: 
    uv sync
    This will automatically create a .venv and install MkDocs with all required plugins based on the uv.lock file.

docs.title: Level 1: Novice (General Members)
docs.text: If you are new to Git or documentation, you can still contribute content: * The Workflow: Draft your project updates, technical specs, or logs in Markdown format. * Submission: Send your .md files or raw text to the Documentation Moderators via the designated Discord channel. They will review, format, and upload the content for you.

docs.title: Level 2: Advanced (Moderators)
docs.text: For members comfortable with version control, use the Pull Method: 1. Sync: Ensure your local environment is up to date with uv sync. 2. Preview: Run uv run mkdocs serve to see changes in real-time at http://localhost:8000. 3. Branch: Create a new feature branch for your edits. 4. PR: Submit a Pull Request on GitHub. A Lead Moderator will review and merge your changes.

docs.title: Level 3: Privileged (Leads/Admin)
docs.text: Members of the CodeHive GitHub Organization with write access can use the Push Method: * The Workflow: Direct push to the main branch is permitted for minor updates, configuration changes, or urgent documentation fixes. * Requirement: Ensure uv run mkdocs serve passes without errors before pushing.

docs.title: ✍️ Writing Documentation
docs.text: To maintain a consistent and professional look across all CodeHive projects, please adhere to the following directory structure and formatting standards.

docs.title: 🏗️ Project Directory Structure
docs.text:
Each project must reside in its own folder under docs/projects/. Follow this exact layout:

docs/projects/[project-name]/
├── assets/             # All images, diagrams, and screenshots
├── pages/              # Deep-dive documentation pages
│   └── [page-name].md
├── index.md            # Project Intro & Getting Started
└── setup.md            # Environment & Infrastructure (Optional)

docs.title: File Descriptions
docs.text:
1. index.md: The landing page for the project. Should include the project's purpose, the lead members, and a "Quick Start" guide.
  2. setup.md: Technical details regarding the environment (e.g., Docker configs, M4 Mac Mini specs, required Python version via uv).
  3. pages/: Any documentation that doesn't fit in the index or setup goes here (e.g., alu-logic.md, api-endpoints.md).
  4. assets/: Always store project-specific images here. Link them using: ![Alt Text](../../assets/[project-name]/image.png)

docs.title: 📖 Formatting Standards
docs.text: We use Material for MkDocs. Refer to the Official Reference for advanced components like tabs, buttons, or math.

docs.title: 1. Code Blocks
docs.text:
Always specify the language for syntax highlighting. - Example:

```yaml
services:
  mc-server:
    image: itzg/minecraft-server
```

docs.title: 2. Admonitions
docs.text:
Use these for callouts, tips, or warnings: 

!!! info "Pro Tip"
    When building with Redstone, use `Carpet Mod` to freeze the tick rate for frame-perfect debugging.

docs.title: 🚀 Navigation & mkdocs.yml
docs.text:
When you add a new file or project, you must update the nav section in the root mkdocs.yml. Use capitalized titles for the navigation labels.

Standard Navigation Template: 

nav:
  - Projects:
      - [Project Name]:
          - Overview: projects/[project-name]/index.md
          - Setup: projects/[project-name]/setup.md
          - [Specific Page Name]: projects/[project-name]/pages/[file].md

docs.title: All the projects:
docs.text: • Minecraft CPU: Build a 8-bit computer in minecraft

docs.title: 🖥️ Minecraft CPU Project: Overview
docs.text: Welcome to the flagship engineering project of CodeHive. We are designing and constructing a functional, programmable 8-bit Computer-on-a-Chip entirely within Minecraft using Redstone logic.

docs.title: 🎯 Project Objective
docs.text: The goal of this project is to bridge the gap between high-level programming and low-level digital electronics. By the end of this cycle, we aim to have a CPU capable of executing a custom instruction set, supported by RAM and a programmable ROM.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• 8-Bit Architecture: Standard 8-bit data bus for arithmetic and logic operations.
  • Modular Design: Separate modules for the ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit), Registers, Control Unit, and Memory.
  • Programmable: A ROM module that can be "flashed" with custom programs using Litematica or in-game tools.
  • High-Performance Hosting: Hosted on a dedicated Mac Mini M4 via Docker for maximum tick-rate stability.

docs.title: 🚀 Getting Started for Members
docs.text:
If you are joining the engineering team, follow these steps to get your environment ready:

  1. Read the Setup Guide: This contains the server IP, the required Mod-stack, and Docker configuration.
  2. Join the Discord: All real-time logic debugging and "WorldEdit" sessions are coordinated in the #mc-cpu-dev channel.
  3. Install Litematica: We use schematics to share circuit designs. Ensure you have the latest blueprints from the assets/ folder.

docs.title: Phase 1: The ALU (Current)
docs.text:
• [ ] 8-bit Adder/Subtractor logic.
  • [ ] Bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT).
  • [ ] Flags register (Zero, Carry, Negative).

docs.title: Phase 2: Memory & Registers
docs.text:
• [ ] 8-byte General Purpose Register file.
  • [ ] 64-byte RAM module.
  • [ ] Program Counter (PC) logic.

docs.title: Phase 3: Control Unit & Instruction Set
docs.text:
• [ ] Instruction Decoder.
  • [ ] Microcode implementation.
  • [ ] Final assembly and "Hello World" program.

docs.title: 👥 Project Contributors
docs.text:
• Project Lead: Yeasin Arafat Rafio
  • Logic Engineers: [Add Member Name]
  • Infrastructure/SysAdmin: [Add Member Name]

Tip: Use the /carpet commands on the server to monitor MSPT (Milliseconds Per Tick). We aim to keep the CPU cycle lag-free even at high clock speeds.

docs.title: ⚙️ Infrastructure & Environment Setup
docs.text:
The development of the CodeHive CPU Project is divided into two distinct stages to ensure stability before adding advanced debugging tools.

  • Phase 1 (Initial): Core logic building and basic server stability.
  • Phase 2 (Advanced): High-frequency debugging, frame-perfect multimeters, and bulk data-bus cloning.

docs.title: 🏗️ Server-Side Configuration (Admins)
docs.text: The Minecraft server is the "Central Processing Unit" of our project. It runs in a containerized environment to ensure high-frequency Redstone calculations remain consistent.

docs.title: Requirements & Environment
docs.text:
• Hardware: Recommended 6GB+ RAM to prevent tick-skip during large logic updates.
  • World Settings: Creative Mode, Flat World, No Mob Spawning.
  • Deployment: Managed via Docker. For those preferring a GUI, SquidServer is a compatible alternative.
  • Networking: Currently utilizing playit.gg for tunneling. > Note: We will transition to direct Port Forwarding in Phase 2 for reduced latency.

docs.title: Server Orchestration
docs.text:
The configuration is maintained in our docker-compose file.

  • Config File: ./assets/minecraft-server.yml

docs.title: Primary Server Mods
docs.text: Mod Phase Purpose Fabric API P1 Essential hook for all server-side mods. Lithium P1 Physics and game-loop optimization. Carpet Mod P1 Tick-rate control and performance monitoring. WorldEdit P1 Rapid prototyping and structure manipulation.

docs.title: 🎮 Client-Side Setup (Players)
docs.text: To connect to the CodeHive cluster, players must configure their local Minecraft installation to match our Fabric environment.

docs.title: Launcher & Version
docs.text:
• Recommended Launcher: SKLauncher (allows for easy modpack management).
  • Minecraft Version: 1.21.1
  • Mod Loader: Fabric

docs.title: Installation Methods
docs.text:
1. Manual / SKLauncher: Go to Installation Manager > Modpacks. Search and add the required mods listed below.
  2. Quick Setup: Download the Client Mod-Stack v0.1 and extract it into your .minecraft/mods folder.

docs.title: Essential Client Mods
docs.text:
• Fabric API: Core dependency.
  • MaLiLib: Library required for Litematica.
  • Litematica: For viewing CPU logic blueprints and schematics.
  • Sodium: Drastic FPS improvements when viewing large-scale Redstone builds.

docs.title: Visual Support
docs.text:
Upon joining the server, you will receive an automatic prompt to download the Redstone Tweaks resource pack.

  • Required: Yes.
  • Function: Visualizes wire signal strengths (0–15) and directional pulse indicators, which are critical for debugging logic gates.

docs.title: All Mods List
docs.text:
This is the master list for the CodeHive CPU Project. It is categorized by phase to ensure we don't overload the server or clients during the initial build.

Mod Name Phase Side Function Fabric API P1 Both Core library required for all mods to function. MaLiLib P1 Client Library mod required for Litematica's UI and configs. Litematica P1 Client Provides holograms/schematics for building CPU components. Carpet Mod P1 Both Allows /tick control, fake players, and TPS/MSPT monitoring. Lithium P1 Server Optimization for physics and game-loop logic. Sodium P1 Client Massive FPS engine overhaul; essential for large Redstone builds. WorldEdit P1 Both Enables bulk cloning, rotating, and moving of large logic buses. Redstone Tweaks P1 Client (Resource Pack) Visualizes power levels (0-15) on wires. RS Multimeter P2 Both Frame-perfect logic debugging and pulse capturing. Axiom P2 Client Professional-grade tool for world manipulation and 3D logic. Iris Shaders P2 Client Used for high-quality cinematic recordings of the finished CPU.

docs.title: Installation Breakdown
docs.text:
• Server-Side: These are installed in the Docker container (./assets/minecraft-server.yml). They ensure the world stays lag-free and supports our logic tools.
  • Client-Side: These go in your local mods folder. If you are using SKLauncher, you can manually add these via the Installation Manager or use our pre-built zip.
  • Both: These mods must be present on both the server and your computer for the features (like WorldEdit commands or Multimeter data) to sync properly.
